content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="perturbetur_de_1"> , <gloss>is alarmed for.</gloss> <milestone n="171.5" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="novi_judici_1">, i.e. the special tribunal established by vote of the people to try all cases arising out of the brawl between Milo and Clodius. On this occasion the court was surrounded, not by the usual throng of spectators, but by an armed guard (see Introd., p. 170 of text), — hence <foreign xml:lang="lat">nova forma</foreign>. <milestone n="171.7" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="requirunt_1"> , <gloss>miss, seek in vain.</gloss> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pro_templis_2">: see plan of Forum. The guards held the entrances to these temples as important strategic points for defence against the mob. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_aliquid_2"> , <gloss>do not fail to bring something</gloss> (of terror or constraint). <milestone n="171.11" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_possimus_2"> , <gloss>so that we cannot even be relieved of fear</gloss> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">non timere</foreign>) <gloss>without some fear</gloss> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="foro_2">, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="judicio_2">: such a display of arms in places especially devoted to occupations of peace must necessarily cause some uneasiness. <pb n="429"/> <milestone n="171.14" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_putarem_2">: Cicero artfully assumes throughout the oration that the authorities (including Pompey) are on the side of Milo. <milestone n="171.16" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="me_recreat_2">: to preserve the emphatic position we may change the voice, —<gloss>I am reassured</gloss>; so </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="reficit_2">, <gloss>I am revived</gloss>. <milestone n="171.17" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="justitiae_suae_2">: § <ref n="AG 343">343</ref>, c (214, d); cf. B. 198, 3; cf. G. 366; cf. H. 439 (401); H.-B. 340; 50 <foreign xml:lang="lat">sapientiae</foreign> in 1.19. <milestone n="171.18" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="putaret_2">, <gloss>would not have thought</gloss>: § <ref n="AG 446">446</ref> (311, a); B. 280, 4; G. 258; H. 552, 554, 2, 3 (485 and N.1); H.-B. 519, 2. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tradidisset_2">, subj. of integral part. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illa_arma_3">, etc.: on the first day of the trial, when M. Marcellus began to cross-examine one of the witnesses against Milo, he was so terrified by the rush of the mob that he took refuge on the <foreign xml:lang="lat"> praetor</foreign>'s bench. Pompey, alarmed by the same disturbance, came down next day with an armed guard, and the trial was allowed to proceed without interruption. <milestone n="171.23" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quieto_3">, etc.: i.e. not only to be undisturbed but to be greatly encouraged. <milestone n="171.25" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quae_civium_3"> , <gloss>so far at least as it consists of citizens</gloss> (covertly suggesting that the supporters of Clodius were not citizens, but gladiators and the like). <milestone n="171.26" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="neque_non_3">, etc.: notice the double negative, — <gloss>there is no one... who does not</gloss>, etc. <milestone n="171.30" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="decertari_3"> (impersonal), <gloss>that the conflict is</gloss>. Here Cicero suggests what one of the chief points of his defence is to be, — that Milo had always acted in defence of the state. <milestone n="172.1" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eorum_3">, <gloss>namely, of those</gloss>: § <ref n="AG 344">344</ref> (214, e); B. 197; G. 368, a.; cf. H. 441 (397, I); H.-B. 349. <milestone n="172.3" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hesterna_contione_3"> , <gloss>yesterday's harangue</gloss>. The day before, after the court adjourned, one T. Munatius Plancus (see sect. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 12">12</ref>) had harangued the crowd, urging them to be on hand next day and not suffer Milo to escape. On this day, the last of the trial, shops were closed throughout the city; Pompey posted guards in the Forum and all its approaches; he himself sat, as on the day before, in front of the Treasury, girt with a select body of troops. When Cicero began to speak, "he was received by an outcry of the party of Clodius, who could not be restrained even by fear of the surrounding soldiery." <milestone n="172.4" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="judicaretis_3">: § <ref n="AG 444">444</ref> (268); B. 277; G. 259; H. 557 (486, ii); H.-B. 503. <milestone n="172.6" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="retineatis_3">: the penalty for the offence with which Milo was charged was banishment, by which he would lose his rights as citizen. <pb n="430"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="locus_4">, <gloss> opportunity</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="amplissimorum_ordinum_4">: the court was made up of Senators, <foreign xml:lang="lat">equites</foreign>, and <foreign xml:lang="lat">tribuni aerarii</foreign>. <milestone n="172.12" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="delectis_4">: the whole body of jurors (360) was selected, though the particular jury (of 51) was drawn by lot. <milestone n="172.14" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="re_sententus_4"> , <gloss>by act and verdict</gloss> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nobis_duobus_5"> , <gloss>than we two</gloss>, i.e. the orator and his client. Cicero constantly associates himself with Milo in this fashion, thus not merely following the custom of advocates, but also representing Milo as engaged in the same kind of opposition to the dangerous elements in the state as that which had made his own consulship illustrious. <milestone n="172.23" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="crudelissimorum_5">: exile was the worst that Milo had to fear. Here Cicero alludes to his own experience of it, which had resulted from his patriotic efforts against the Catilinarian conspirators. <milestone n="172.24" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ceteras_5">, etc.: it was to be expected that a politician should undergo abuse and even illegal violence in the stormy public life of the time, but such dangers were not to be anticipated in an impartial court. <milestone n="172.27" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ex_ordinibus_5">: see note on <foreign xml:lang="lat">ordinum</foreign>, sect. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 4">4</ref> (<ref n="pl172.11">p. 172, l. 11</ref>). <milestone n="172.29" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="salutem_5">, i.e. not his personal safety in the modern sense, but his political rights (see note on 1.23, above). <milestone n="172.30" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="talis_viros_5">: it was admitted, says Asconius, that no body of jurors had ever been more illustrious or just than those who composed this court. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quamquam_6"> , <gloss>and yet</gloss> (corrective). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tribunatu_6">: in the year <date when="-0057">B.C. 57</date> Milo, as tribune, had materially assisted in procuring the recall of Cicero from banishment. It was partly gratitude for this service that led Cicero to undertake Milo's defence. <milestone n="173.3" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="abutemur_6"> , <gloss>take unfair advantage of</gloss> Cicero declines to use Milo's services to the state as an argument in his favor, and boldly declares that he will put the case on the bare facts, contending that Milo acted in self-defence. But in the very act of declining to dwell on these services he subtly emphasizes them, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fuerit_7">, subj. of integral part. <milestone n="173.13" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ad_orationem_7"> , <gloss>to that line of argument</gloss> <milestone n="173.14" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="videntur_7">, etc.: § <ref n="AG 582">582</ref> (330, b, 1); B. 332, b; G. 528, R.2; H. 611, N.1, 2 (534, i, N.1) cf. H.-B. 590, I and a. <milestone n="173.20" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="primum_7">, i.e. the first capital trial that occurred in Rome. <milestone n="173.21" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="M_Horati_7">: the famous story of the three Horatii and the three Curiatii. When Horatius was condemned to death for the murder of his sister, he was acquitted on appeal to the people; and this incident passed as the origin of <foreign xml:lang="lat">provocatio</foreign>, or appeal to the people from the decision of a magistrate. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nondum_libera_7">, i.e. under the kings: it was in the reign of Tullus Hostilius, <date when="-0668">B.C. 668</date>. <milestone n="173.22" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="comitiis_7">, i.e. by the vote of the Roman people assembled to decide the appeal. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="P_Africanum_8">, i.e. <foreign xml:lang="lat">Aemilianus</foreign>: he was cousin (by adoption) and brother-in-law of Gracchus, and friendly to the spirit of his reforms, although not sympathizing with his violent course. <milestone n="173.27" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="C_Carbone_8">: C. Carbo was an unscrupulous politician who supported Ti. Gracchus, but afterwards was a bitter antagonist of C. Gracchus. <pb n="432"/> <milestone n="173.30" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ahala_8">, etc.: these are cases which would have to be called infamous murders unless the principle that homicide is sometimes justifiable were admitted. The instances referred to were stock examples in Roman oratory (see Cat. 1, sects. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 3">3</ref>, <ref n="Cic. Mil. 4">4</ref>), though, in fact, Ahala and the others were all driven into exile by a reaction of popular feeling. <milestone n="173.31" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="senatus_8">: because the execution of the Catilinarian conspirators was by vote of the Senate. <milestone n="174.2" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fictis_fabulis_8">, properly, <gloss>mythical dramas</gloss>: the reference is to the <foreign xml:lang="lat">Eumenides</foreign> of Aeschylus, which treats of the expiation of the guilt of Orestes, son of Agamemnon, at the court of Areopagus in Athens. When six judges had pronounced for condemnation and six for acquittal, Pallas gave her casting vote for mercy. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="doctissimi_8">, i.e. the greatest poets. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="duodecim_tabulae_9">: the "Twelve Tables" were the code which formed the basis of Roman law, drawn up <date when="-0451">B.C. 451</date> by an elected board of ten commissioners (<foreign xml:lang="lat">decemviri</foreign>). This codification of the laws continued in force, and was the starting point of the legal education of every Roman, and of all later development of Roman law. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quoquo_modo_9"> , <gloss>under any circumstances</gloss>. <milestone n="174.8" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quoquo_modo_9_2"> , <gloss>in whatever way</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quis_9"> (indefinite), <gloss>one</gloss>; the preceding <foreign xml:lang="lat"> quis</foreign> is interrogative. <milestone n="174.13" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vi_defenditur_9"> , <gloss>violence offered is repelled by force. </gloss>-<foreign xml:lang="lat">pudicitiam</foreign> . . . <foreign xml:lang="lat"> eriperet</foreign>, <gloss>tried to rob of his honor</gloss>. <milestone n="174.14" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tribunus_9">: C. Lusius, son of Marius's sister. This was a stock instance among rhetoricians in arguing the just limits of self-defence. <milestone n="174.17" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="scelere_solutum_9"> , <gloss>acquitted of guilt</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vero_10">: introducing, as usual, a strong case. <milestone n="174.19" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="comitatus_10"> , <gloss>body-guards</gloss>, which would seem to have been a common thing among these gentlemen of Rome, as among the partisan chieftains of the Middle Ages. <milestone n="174.20" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="volunt_10">, <gloss> mean</gloss>. <milestone n="174.20" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="adripuimus_10"> , <gloss>caught</gloss>; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hausimus_10"> , <gloss>imbibed</gloss>; <foreign xml:lang="lat">expressimus</foreign>, <gloss>wrought out</gloss>; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="imbuti_10">, <gloss> steeped</gloss>. <milestone n="175.1" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_esset_10">, purpose clause after <foreign xml:lang="lat">lex</foreign> (a word of decreeing). <milestone n="175.2" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="incidisset_10">: in the words of the <foreign xml:lang="lat">lex</foreign>, - <foreign xml:lang="lat">incident</foreign>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sflent_11">: notice the emphatic position. <milestone n="175.4" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="velit_11">: subjunctive because of sit. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ante_quam_11">: § <ref n="AG 434">434</ref> (262); G. 574, R.1; H. (p.293, footnote 1); cf. 14.-B. 507, 4, footnote 1 <milestone n="175.5" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="etsi_11">, i.e. there is no need for me to appeal to the law of nature to establish the right to kill in self-defence, for this right is established <pb n="433"/> by the judicial interpretation of a particular statute (of Sulla). This statute (<foreign xml:lang="lat">de sicariis</foreign>) dealt with murder in general and went so far as to provide a penalty for the carrying of a weapon with intent to kill. The courts had ruled, says Cicero, that, under this law, a man who carried a weapon for self-defence was not carrying it <foreign xml:lang="lat">hominis occidendi causa</foreign>, in the meaning of the statute. <milestone n="175.10" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="judicaretur_11">: the subject is antecedent of <foreign xml:lang="lat">qui</foreign>. <milestone n="175.11" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hoc_causa_11"> , <gloss>let this point stand as the law of the case</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sequitur_illud_12"> , <gloss>the next point is this</gloss>: § <ref n="AG 597">597</ref>, d, i (344, d, I); Cf. H.-B. 625. <milestone n="175.16" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="contra_factam_12">: a technical phrase, amounting to "unlawful (unconstitutional) violence"; cf. our "a breach of the peace." </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illam_12">, i.e. <foreign xml:lang="lat">caedem</foreign>. <milestone n="175.17" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sententiis_12">, .e. its expressed vote; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="studiis_12">, i.e. the interest it displayed in Milo's behalf? <milestone n="175.19" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nec_tacitis_12"> , <gloss>loudly</gloss>; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nec_occultis_12"> , <gloss>in plain terms</gloss>. <milestone n="176.1" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="declarant_12">: to preserve the emphasis we may change the voice, — <gloss>it is shown by</gloss>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hujus_tribuni_12">: the body of Clodius, left in the highway, had been picked up and sent to Rome, where its wounds were exposed to public gaze till, in the fury of the time, it was dragged to the Senate-house. Here a funeral-pile was made of desks, benches, and other furniture, and in the conflagration the Senate-house itself, with several other buildings, was destroyed. The tribune T. Munatius Plancus, who incited the mob to burn the body, is called <foreign xml:lang="lat">ambustus</foreign>, <gloss> fire-scorched</gloss>, because his influence suffered in the reaction of public feeling which followed the unintended conflagration; hence, too, his <gloss>harangues</gloss> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">contiones</foreign>) are referred to as <gloss>still-born</gloss> (<foreign xml:lang="lat"> intermortuae</foreign>). <milestone n="176.2" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="potentiam_12"> , <gloss>unlawful domination</gloss>. <milestone n="176.6" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="officiosos_12"> , <gloss>serviceable</gloss>, in the way of forensic advocacy. <milestone n="176.8" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sane_12">, <gloss>if you like; or for aught I care</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vero_13">: in reference to the statement at the beginning of sect. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 12">12</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hanc_quaestionem_13"> , <gloss>this special court</gloss> <milestone n="176.14" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cujus_13">: observe that the relative precedes the antecedent (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ejus_13">), as often in Latin. There is no antecedent noun ("man," "person") expressed. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="de_stupro_13"> , <gloss>that incestuous outrage</gloss>: this refers to a frightful scandal when the mysteries of the <foreign xml:lang="lat">Bona Dea</foreign> (a rite held solely by women) were profaned by Clodius's introducing himself in female attire. The mysteries were being held at the official residence of Caesar, then <foreign xml:lang="lat">pontifex maximus</foreign>, and the latter's wife Pompeia was thought to have connived at the intrusion. Caesar affected to believe no harm, but presently divorced Pompeia, with the famous remark that "Caesar's wife must be above suspicion." Being brought to trial, Clodius tried to prove an <foreign xml:lang="lat">alibi</foreign>, but this attempt was frustrated, in part by the testimony of Cicero. A corrupt jury acquitted Clodius, but he never forgave Cicero for appearing as a witness against him. <milestone n="176.15" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="erepta_13">: a special investigation had been ordered on account of the religious importance of the case; hence the decision was <gloss>taken away from</gloss> the Senate. <milestone n="176.17" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cur_igitur_13">, etc.: Cicero puts himself for the moment in the place of the other side and himself asks the question which an objector might put. He answers this question in <foreign xml:lang="lat">quia nulla</foreign>, etc. (1.19). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="incendium_curiae_13">: see note on sect. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 12">12</ref> (p. 176, 1. I), above. <milestone n="176.18" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Lepidi_13">: after the death of Clodius, M. Aemilius Lepidus (afterwards triumvir with Octavianus and Antony) had been appointed <foreign xml:lang="lat"> interrex</foreign>, a formality necessary to give regularity to the forms of election when there were no consuls (see Introd. to this Oration, p.170 of text). In the disorders that followed his house was stormed and plundered by the mob. <milestone n="176.20" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_contra_13">, [which is] <gloss>not</gloss>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illa_defensio_14"> , <gloss>such a defence</gloss>, i.e. the defence by violence (cf. <foreign xml:lang="lat">vi vis defenditur</foreign>, sect. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 9">9</ref>). <milestone n="176.24" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="e_publica_14"> , <gloss>in the interest of the commonwealth</gloss>. <milestone n="177.1" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="decrevi_14">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">notavi</foreign>, <gloss>I voted, I marked</gloss>, i.e. as deserving punishment, leaving the person of the criminal to the decision of the court (sect. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 31">31</ref>). These words refer to Cicero's acts and votes in the Senate when Pompey's law was under discussion (see note on 1.8). <milestone n="177.3" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="crimen_14">: <gloss>the charge</gloss> against any particular person; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="rem_14">, <gloss>the act itself</gloss> <milestone n="177.5" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tribunum_14">: Plancus. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="licuisset_14">: the action was stayed by the tribune's <foreign xml:lang="lat"> intercessio</foreign>. <milestone n="177.6" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="decernebat_14"> , <gloss>it was on the point of deciding:</gloss> § <ref n="AG 471">471</ref>, e (277, c); B. 260, 3; G. 233; H. 530 (469, ii'); cf. H.-B. 484. <milestone n="177.7" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="extra_ordinem_14"> , <gloss>out of turn</gloss>: i.e. that they should have precedence of the regular docket, but that no new provisions should be made for an investigation. <milestone n="177.8" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="divisa_est_14"> , <gloss>the question was divided</gloss>. When Pompey's proposed law <foreign xml:lang="lat">de vi</foreign>, establishing a special tribunal (<foreign xml:lang="lat">hanc quaestionem</foreign>, sect. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 13">13</ref>) for the investigation of the disturbances referred to, was discussed in the Senate, a resolution opposed to it was offered. This consisted of two clauses: (I) that the disturbances were <foreign xml:lang="lat">contra rem publicam</foreign>, and (2) that cases arising out of them should be brought in the regular courts, but should be advanced on the docket (<foreign xml:lang="lat"> veteribus</foreign> —<foreign xml:lang="lat">legibus</foreign>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">extra ordinem</foreign>). The tribune Q. Fufius Calenus (whom Cicero refuses to name: <foreign xml:lang="lat">nescio quo</foreign>) demanded that the question be divided. The first clause was then passed, whereupon the tribune Plancus interposed his veto (<foreign xml:lang="lat">empta intercessione</foreign>, l.10) and prevented the second from being voted upon. Thus it was made to appear that the Senate approved the new tribunal, and Pompey's law was immediately passed by the public assembly, while the more cautious action of the Senate (<foreign xml:lang="lat">reliqua auctoritas</foreign>) was blocked by the <foreign xml:lang="lat">intercessio</foreign> of Plancus. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="at_enim_15">: introducing another supposed objection of Cicero's opponents. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="rogatione_15">, i.e. when he brought his law <foreign xml:lang="lat">de vi</foreign> (just referred to) before the people for enactment (the proceedings in the Senate having been merely deliberative). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="re_15">, <gloss>the facts</gloss> of the affray; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="causa_15">, <gloss>the case</gloss> of the accused. <milestone n="177.12" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quae_esset_15">: § <ref n="AG 592">592</ref>, 3 (341, d); B. 323; G. 628; H. 649, I (528, i); H.-B. 535, I, a. <milestone n="177.14" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nempe_15">, etc., <gloss>simply. that an investigation should be made</gloss>. <milestone n="177.16" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="juris_defensionem_15"> , <gloss>a defence on a point of law</gloss>, (as to justification of the homicide). <milestone n="177.19" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hanc_litteram_15"> , <gloss>this saving letter</gloss> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">hanc</foreign>, because in favor of his client), i.e. a chance to acquit. Each juror inscribed on his ballot A (<foreign xml:lang="lat">absolvo</foreign>) for acquittal, or C or K (<foreign xml:lang="lat">condemno</foreign>) for conviction. Fig. 44 shows (on the reverse) a voting urn and a ballot marked A and C. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Publione_16">, etc., i.e. whether his act (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_16">, <gloss> what</gloss>) was something he owed to Clodius, or a concession to the exigencies of the time, which demanded the investigation, <pb n="436"/> <milestone n="177.27" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="domi_16">, etc.: the following are examples of assassination in which no extraordinary tribunal (commissioned for investigation) was established. They are cited in support of Cicero's contention that in establishing such a tribunal in the present case Pompey was not pre-judging the innocence of Clodius and consequent guilt of Milo, but merely yielding to the necessities of the public situation. <milestone n="177.29" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Catonis_16">: M. Porcius Cato (the Younger); see note on Archias, sect. <ref n="Cic. Arch. 22">22</ref> (<ref n="pl164.8">p. 164, l. 8</ref>). <milestone n="177.30" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Drusus_16">: M. Livius Drusus (;on of Marcus) was murdered by some unknown person on returning home from an exciting political debate (<date when="-0091">B.C. 91</date>). <milestone n="178.3" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Africano_16">, i.e. Aemilianus. He was actively opposed to the plans of C. Gracchus for the division of the Latian lands; and, while the controversy was at its hottest, was found dead in his bed with marks (it was thought) of strangulation. His wife, sister of the tribune, and Gracchus himself lay under some suspicion of the crime, which was probably the act of Carbo (see note to sect. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 8">8</ref>, <ref n="pl173.27">p. 173, l. 27</ref>). <milestone n="178.4" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quem_immortalem_16">, etc.: Scipio was murdered at the age of fifty-six. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="intersit_17">: § <ref n="AG 440">440</ref> (266, c); B. 308; G. 264; H. 559, 3 (484 111) H B 532, I. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="summorum_17"> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">infimorum</foreign>, simply <gloss>high</gloss> and <gloss>low</gloss>. <milestone n="178.10" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quidem_17">, <gloss> yet</gloss> <milestone n="178.13" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="monumentis_17"> , <gloss> <foreign xml:lang="lat">memoria</foreign>4</gloss> i.e. the road itself. The Appian Way was constructed <date when="-0312">B.C. 312</date> by the censor Ap. Claudius Caecus, an ancestor of Clodius. This circumstance is skillfully used to tell against Clodius, rather than in his favor. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="M_Papirium_18">: this was one of Clodius's earliest exploits. Papirius, a friend of Pompey, was killed in a brawl about a son of Tigranes, held as hostage at Rome, whom Clodius was trying to rescue and send back for a great ransom to Asia, having by a trick got him out of the hands of his custodian. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_fuit_18">, etc.: an illustration of the idea expressed in <foreign xml:lang="lat"> impune</foreign>, 1.16, above. <milestone n="178.21" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quae_18">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">te</foreign>. the Appian Way. <milestone n="178.25" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="templo_Castoris_18">, where the Senate was then holding session. The circumstance took place in the year of Clodius's tribunate (<date when="-0058">B.C. 58</date>), <pb n="437"/> while Pompey was in the Senate. "he instantly went home and stayed there." </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="num_quae_19"> , <gloss>was there any</gloss>, etc. <milestone n="178.31" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="haec_19">, i.e. <foreign xml:lang="lat">res</foreign>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">vir</foreign> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">tempus</foreign>. <milestone n="178.32" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="summa_19"> (pred. to <foreign xml:lang="lat">omnia</foreign>), <gloss>in the highest degree</gloss>. <milestone n="179.5" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="proinde_quasi_19"> , <gloss>just as if</gloss> etc.: § <ref n="AG 524">524</ref>(312); B. 307; G. 602; H. 584,2 (513, ii, N.1); H.-B. 504,3. That is, the <gloss>overt act</gloss> must be judged by its obvious intent. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="luget_20">, etc.: the whole description is, with intentional irony, in lively contrast with the real facts. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_fuit_21">, etc., <gloss>that was not the reason</gloss>, contradicting the ironical statements just made; the real reason is given in the clause introduced by <foreign xml:lang="lat">sed</foreign>. <milestone n="180.6" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cur_censeret_21">: § <ref n="AG 575">575</ref>, b (334, b); B. 300, 2; G. 467; cf. H. 642,3(523, ii, I,N.); H-B. 513, 2. <milestone n="180.7" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ferendam_21"> , <gloss>should be</gloss> (proposed to the people to be) <gloss>voted</gloss> . <milestone n="180.10" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="reconciliatue_21">: Pompey had lately renewed friendly relations with Clodius. <milestone n="180.13" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="delegit_21">: the choice of the <gloss> <foreign xml:lang="lat">judices</foreign> </gloss> was left to Pompey. <milestone n="180.15" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="neque_cogitavit_21"> , <gloss>he had no such idea</gloss>. <milestone n="180.17" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_21">, etc., <gloss>for my influence is not limited to my personal friendships</gloss> . <milestone n="180.22" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="potuit_non_21"> , <gloss>could not but</gloss> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_22">, <gloss>in that</gloss>: § <ref n="AG 572">572</ref>, a (333, a); B. 299, 2 ; G. 525, 2 ; H. 588, 31 N. (516, 2, N.); H-B. 552, 2. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Domiti_22">: L. Domitius Ahenobarbus (consul, <date when="-0054">B.C. 54</date>), afterwards a leader against Caesar in the Civil War, an arrogant and uncompromising upholder of the aristocracy. The emperor Nero was his descendant. <milestone n="180.25" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="consularem_22">: sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">praeesse</foreign>. <milestone n="180.29" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="documenta_maxima_22">: in his praetorship (<date when="-0058">B.C. 58</date>) Domitius had roughly cut his way through a crowd of the followers of Clodius, killing many of them. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_neque_23">, etc.: a recapitulation of the whole of the <foreign xml:lang="lat"> Confutatio</foreign> (sects. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 7">7</ref>-<ref n="Cic. Mil. 22">22</ref>). <milestone n="181.2" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vellemus_23">: § <ref n="AG 447">447</ref>, 1 (311, b); B. 280, 4; G. 258; H. 556 (486, i); H.-B. 519, I, b. <milestone n="181.6" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="uter_utri_23"> , <gloss>which against the other</gloss> (lit. <gloss>which against which</gloss>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_praetura_24">: at the time of his death Clodius was candidate for this office (see Introd. to the oration). <milestone n="181.12" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_mensis_24">: really less than six. At whatever time the magistrates were elected, they could hold office only for the year for which they were chosen. In this case, since the election was delayed till long after the beginning of the year, the term of office was very considerably shortened. <milestone n="181.13" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="honoris_gradum_24">: the ambition of a Roman was to complete the series of <foreign xml:lang="lat">curule</foreign> offices (<foreign xml:lang="lat">cursus honorum</foreign>) as speedily as possible. <milestone n="181.16" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="annum_suum_24"> , <gloss>the regular year</gloss> in which he could legally be a candidate. <milestone n="181.17" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="religione_aliqun_24"> , <gloss>from some religious scruple, as it is generally (Ut fit</gloss> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="consule_Milone_25">: equiv. to a fut. <foreign xml:lang="lat">protasis</foreign>; § <ref n="AG 52">52</ref>!, a (310, a); B. 305, I , G. 600, I; H. 575, 9 (507, N.7); H.-B. 578, 6. Milo was a candidate for the consulship. <milestone n="181.22" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fieri_25">, <gloss>was likely to be elected</gloss> (i.e. as things were going at the time of speaking, hence the pres.). <milestone n="181.25" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="convocabat_25">, not officially, but in the course of his canvass. <milestone n="181.26" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Collinam_novam_25"> , <gloss>a new Colline tribe</gloss>. Of the thirty-five tribes, the four city tribes ranked lowest, because the freedmen and poor citizens were placed in them; and of these the <foreign xml:lang="lat">Collina</foreign> was least reputable of all. It was through the <foreign xml:lang="lat">collegia compitalicia</foreign>, or local clubs, that Clodius worked upon the city tribes; and, by the exaggerated expression that he registered an entirely new <foreign xml:lang="lat">Collina</foreign>, it appears to be meant that the new and perhaps fraudulent names that he got upon the list outnumbered the genuine voters. <milestone n="181.27" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ille_25">, Clodius; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hic_25">, Milo (as generally in this speech). <milestone n="181.31" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="suffragiis_25">: there had already been several attempts to elect magistrates, which had failed through the obstructive tricks familiar to Roman politicians. Hence the preference of the citizens was already well known <gloss>by their votes</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sflvas_publicas_26">: probably some depredations of Clodius in Etruria, where he had extensive estates. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sollemne_27"> , <gloss> <foreign xml:lang="lat">annua</foreign>4</gloss> or occurring at regular seasons (see derivation in Vocab.). The adjectives indicate that Milo's journey was undertaken regularly, legally, and of necessity, and hence that the idea that he went out to kill Clodius is absurd. <milestone n="182.12" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Lanuvium_27">: this was an old town of Latium, about twenty miles southeast of Rome. It contained a temple of Juno Sospita, a local divinity, so famous that, when Lanuvium became a <foreign xml:lang="lat">municipium</foreign> of Rome, this sanctuary was, by special arrangement, received into the Roman religious system. The <foreign xml:lang="lat">flamen</foreign>, or special priest, of Juno Sospita had to be inaugurated by the chief magistrate (<foreign xml:lang="lat">dictator</foreign>) of the <foreign xml:lang="lat">municipium</foreign>. Milo, of Lanuvian origin, a <foreign xml:lang="lat">municeps</foreign> of the town, now held the latter office. <milestone n="182.13" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dictator_27">: this word, evidently an old name for the highest magistrate of a community, was in Rome applied to an extraordinary magistrate, but in other Italian towns it retained its earlier signification (as here). <milestone n="182.16" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ita_27">, <gloss>under such circumstances</gloss>: an indication that Clodius sacrificed something of importance (namely, his presence at an assembly of the people) in order to lay this ambuscade for Milo. Milo, on the other hand (<foreign xml:lang="lat">autem</foreign>), was going about his regular business, as narrated in what follows. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quoad_28">, etc.: the Senate adjourned on this day about the fourth hour (between ten and eleven A.M.). <milestone n="182.21" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="calceos_28">: the Senator wore a special kind of shoe adorned with a crescent-shaped ornament (<foreign xml:lang="lat">lunula</foreign>); his tunic was also distinguished by the broad purple stripe in front (<foreign xml:lang="lat">latus clavus</foreign>). When travelling, a Roman put off his <foreign xml:lang="lat">toga</foreign> and badges of office and put on a heavy travelling cloak (<foreign xml:lang="lat">paenula</foreign>) and other easy garments. <pb n="440"/> <milestone n="182.22" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="id_temporis_28">: § <ref n="AG 397">397</ref>, a (240, b); B. 185, 2; G. 336, N.2; H. 416, 2 (378, 2); H.-B. 388, b. <milestone n="182.24" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ob_fit_28">: this was just beyond Bovillie (Albano), a village about nine miles from Rome. <milestone n="182.25" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="raeda_28"> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">rheda</foreign>), a four-wheeled family carriage. <milestone n="182.26" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Graecis_comitibus_28">, singers, dancers, etc. (see sect. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 55">55</ref>). <milestone n="182.27" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hic_insidiator_28">, i.e. Milo (of course ironical). <milestone n="182.28" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="apparasset_28">, i.e. as the accusers say: see § <ref n="AG 592">592</ref>, 3 (341, d); B. 323; G. 628; H. 649, i (528, I); H.-B. 535, I, a. <milestone n="182.29" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="paenulatus_28">: the <foreign xml:lang="lat">paenula</foreign> went on over the head, like a Mexican poncho, and so confined the arms (Fig. 47). It was the usual travelling cloak of a Roman. <milestone n="182.30" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="comitatu_28">: this troop of singing boys and maidens was, no doubt, to take part in the village procession next day at Lanuvium. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hora_undecima_29">: this would be about half past four P.M. In reality, as we learn from other sources, it was nearly two hours earlier; and Milo had stopped at an inn in Bovillae, in order (as was charged) to make sure of not missing his enemy. <milestone n="183.1" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="adversi_occidunt_29"> , <gloss>they attack and kilt</gloss> <milestone n="183.8" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pugnari_29">, impersonal. <milestone n="183.9" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="succurrere_29">: § <ref n="AG 558">558</ref>, b (331, C, 2); cf. B. 295, 31 N.; G. 549, N.1; H. 505, ii, 2; H.-B. 587. <milestone n="183.10" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fecerunt_voluisset_29">: this sentence is greatly admired as a delicate way of glossing over awkward facts. <milestone n="183.11" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="derivandi_29">, i.e. from Milo, the master, to the irresponsible slaves. <milestone n="183.14" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="voluisset_29">: for constr., see § <ref n="AG 521">521</ref>, a (310, a) ; B. 305, I ; G. 600, I; H. 595, 9 (507, N.7); H.-B. 578, 6. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="consecuta_sit_30">, i.e. by the death of Clodius. <milestone n="183.19" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="prosit_30">, hortatory subjunctive. <milestone n="183.20" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="potuerit_30">: for tense, see § <ref n="AG 485">485</ref>, c (287, c) ; B. 268, 6 G. 513; H. 550 (495, vi); H.-B. 481. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quin_servaret_30"> , <gloss>without saving</gloss>; cf. <foreign xml:lang="lat">quin judicetis</foreign>, <gloss>without judging</gloss> (1.26, below). <milestone n="183.22" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ratio_doctis_30">, etc.: observe the exquisite skill with which Cicero here enunciates and applies the universal law of self-defence. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Observe_section_31"> (sect. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 31">31</ref>), which ends the <foreign xml:lang="lat">Narratio</foreign> (or statement of facts), is similar to sect. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 23">23</ref>, which introduces the <foreign xml:lang="lat">Narratio</foreign>: both contain a summing up of the results arrived at in the <foreign xml:lang="lat">Confutatio</foreign> (sects. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 7">7</ref>-<ref n="Cic. Mil. 22">22</ref>) and both end with a statement of the main question: Which lay in wait for the other? Much of the effort of persuasive argument depends on such repetitions, at the proper moment, of points already made. <milestone n="183.28" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="optabilius_fuit_31">: § <ref n="AG 522">522</ref>, a (311, c); B. 304, 3, a; G. 254, R.1; H. 583 (511, N.1); H.-B. 582, 3, a. <milestone n="183.29" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="semel_31">, <gloss>once only</gloss>. <milestone n="183.32" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illud_31">, referring forward to <foreign xml:lang="lat">occisusne</foreign>, etc. (as often). <milestone n="184.1" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="id_31">, i.e. the fact that there was a plot laid by somebody. Throughout the argument Cicero insists that neither the Senate nor Pompey intended to prejudge the guilt of Milo. <milestone n="184.3" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="de_hoc_31">, i.e. the question <foreign xml:lang="lat">ab utro factae sint</foreign>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="latum_est_31">, etc., i.e. this was the intent of Pompey's law <foreign xml:lang="lat">de vi</foreign>. <milestone n="184.7" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hic_31">, i.e. my client; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ille_31">, i.e. Clodius. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_sit_31">, subj. of purpose (i.e. the purpose of the investigation). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="probari_32">, etc.: the evidence on this point would, of course, be purely circumstantial and inferential, and to Cicero's wonderful skill in dealing with such evidence is due much of the interest of what follows. <milestone n="184.10" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_illa_32">, <gloss>in the case of the</gloss>, etc. <milestone n="184.13" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fllud_Cassianum_32"> , <gloss>that noted saying of Cassius</gloss>. L. Cassius Longinus Ravilla (cos. <date when="-0127">B.C. 127</date>) was one of the most upright men of his time, distinguished as a <foreign xml:lang="lat"> quaesitor</foreign> (presiding officer) of special trials. <pb n="442"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cui_bono_32"> , <gloss>for whose advantage</gloss>: § <ref n="AG 382">382</ref>, I and N.1 (233, a); B. 191, 2; G. 356, R.1; H. 433 (390, i); H.-B. 360 and b; not "for what advantage," as it is often wrongly given in English. The question of "motive" in cases of murder was as much insisted on in ancient trials as it is today (cf. Rosc. Am., note at. 23, p. 10, 1. 16). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="personis_32"> , <gloss>parties</gloss>: the persona is properly the <gloss>mask</gloss>, which indicates by its features the "character" in a play. <milestone n="184.15" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="atqui_32">, etc., <gloss>now, by the killing of Milo</gloss>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="adsequebatur_32"> , <gloss>was going to gain</gloss>: § <ref n="AG 471">471</ref>, c (277,c); B. 260,3; G. 233; H. 534, 2 (469, I); cf. H-B. 484. <milestone n="184.16" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_consule_32"> , <gloss>without having one as consul</gloss>. <milestone n="184.18" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quibus_coniventibus_32">: these competitors of Milo were P. Plautius Hypsaeus and Q. Metellus Scipio, — the latter an adopted son of Metellus Pius. He took a leading part on Pompey's side in the Civil War, and was defeated by Caesar at Thapsus, <date when="-0046">B.C. 46</date>. <milestone n="184.19" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="speraret_32">: integral part of the purpose clause </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_esset_32">: so also </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cuperent_32"> , </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vellent_32">, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="possent_32">, below. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cogitatis_32">: artfully suggesting that the mad conduct of Clodius was not mere hot-headedness but was deliberately planned to further his ambitious designs. <milestone n="184.20" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illi_32">, i.e. the consuls. <milestone n="184.21" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tantum_beneficium_32">: they would owe their election to him. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ille_33">, i.e. Clodius. <milestone n="184.28" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fuerit_impositurus_33">: indir. quest. for <foreign xml:lang="lat">-turus fuit</foreign> = <foreign xml:lang="lat">imposuisset</foreign>; § <ref n="AG 517">517</ref>, d (308, d and N.); B. 304, 3, b, 322, b; G. 597, R5, a; H. 582, 2 (511, 2, N.); H-B. 581, b, 2. <milestone n="184.29" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Clodi_33">: Sex. Clodius, client and confidential agent of the demagogue Clodius. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="librarium_33">, i.e. the "budget" of laws which Clodius had on hand to propose. Cicero humorously speaks as if these filled a whole <foreign xml:lang="lat"> librarium</foreign> or book cabinet. <milestone n="184.30" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eripuisse_domo_33">, i.e. from P. Clodius's house, in the riots that followed his death. <milestone n="184.31" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Palladium_33">: the image of Pallas, kept in the citadel of Troy, and taken thence by a nocturnal enterprise of Ulysses and Diomed. The sanctity and adventures of this portfolio suggest the comparison. <milestone n="185.2" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_esses_33"> , <gloss>if you could find him</gloss>. <milestone n="185.3" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="per_33">: the words of adjuration are either intentionally omitted or lost. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hujus_legis_33">: a proposed law of Clodius by which the freedmen were to be distributed among all the thirty-five tribes (see note, sect. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 25"> 25</ref>, p. 181, 1.26). Sex. Clodius, the son of a freedman, is shrewdly hinted at as author of the law. <milestone n="185.5" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="de_omnium_33">: such a rhetorical break is called <foreign xml:lang="lat">aposiopesis</foreign>. Cicero would have said something like <foreign xml:lang="lat"> proscriptione</foreign> or <foreign xml:lang="lat">caede</foreign>, but he affects to be alarmed at the threatening look with which Sex. Clodius hears his allusion (<foreign xml:lang="lat">aspexit</foreign> me <foreign xml:lang="lat">illis oculis</foreign>). <milestone n="185.9" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="lumen_curiae_33">, a pun: Cicero calls Sex. Clodius <gloss>a light of the senate-house</gloss>, meaning (1) sarcastically, that he was a distinguished Senator, and (2) that he was the <gloss>incendiary</gloss> who, by burning Clodius's body, had set the <foreign xml:lang="lat">curia</foreign> on fire (see note on sect. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 12">12</ref>, <ref n="pl176.1">p. 176, l. 1</ref>). <milestone n="185.11" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="poenitus_33"> [= <foreign xml:lang="lat">punitus</foreign>] <foreign xml:lang="lat">es</foreign> (often <foreign xml:lang="lat">deponent</foreign> in Cicero): nothing was more horrible to the ancients than the loss of due funeral rites. The burning of Clodius's body by the mob deprived him of all the honors to which he was entitled. <milestone n="185.12" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="erat_33">: § <ref n="AG 522">522</ref>, a (311, c); B. 304, 3, a; G. 254, p.1; H. 583 (511, N.1); H-B. 582, 3, a. <milestone n="185.14" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="imaginibus_33">: a Claudius should have a long line of most distinguished images. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="infelicissimis_33"> , <gloss>ill-omened</gloss>, as obtained by riotous violence. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="audistis_34">, etc.: the words in italics are supplied by conjecture. <milestone n="185.22" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_dicam_34">: parenthetical. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="obstabat_34">: the supposed remark of an opponent. <milestone n="185.23" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="repugnante_eo_34"> , <gloss>in spite of his opposition</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fiebat_34"> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">se consul</foreign>), <gloss>was coming to be</gloss> (see note <foreign xml:lang="lat">onfieri</foreign>, sect. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 25">25</ref>, p. iii, 1.22). <milestone n="185.24" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nec_me_34">, etc., i.e. the positive support of Cicero was not more useful to Milo's canvass than the antagonism of so bad a man as Clodius. <milestone n="185.25" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="apud_vos_34">, i.e. you and such men as you in your capacity as electors. <milestone n="186.1" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quis_dubitaret_34"> <gloss>who could</gloss> [then] <gloss>hesitate?</gloss> § <ref n="AG 444">444</ref> (268); B. 277; G; 259; H. 557 (486, ii); H-B. 517, I. <milestone n="186.3" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Clodio_remoto_34">, i.e. s9 long as Clodius was alive many would vote for Milo as being his declared enemy, but now that Clodius k out of the way, Milo must rely only on the ordinary means (<foreign xml:lang="lat">usitatis</foreign> jam rebus) of securing suffrages. <milestone n="186.7" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ne_metueretis_34">: § <ref n="AG 537">537</ref>, a, N. (319, a, N.); G. 553, I; cf. H.-B. 502, 3, a, footnote1. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="at_etc_35"> , <gloss>but</gloss> (some one will say). <milestone n="186.14" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="haec_35">, <gloss>these considerations</gloss>. <pb n="444"/> <milestone n="186.18" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="civile_35">, <gloss> political</gloss> (such as a good citizen must feel). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ille_odisset_35">, hi 1115 <gloss>case there was a motive for hating</gloss> (a purpose clause after <foreign xml:lang="lat">erat</foreign> <foreign xml:lang="lat">= causa erat</foreign>). <milestone n="186.21" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="reus_fuit_35">, i.e. liable to an accusation on the part of Milo. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="lege_Plotia_35"> (or Plautia): the Roman statute against assault. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nihil_36">, etc.: of course ironical. <milestone n="186.28" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quid_ego_36">, etc.: Cicero gives his own experience as an example of Clodius's violent way of acting. <milestone n="186.31" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="diem_36">, etc.: example of legal proceedings which Cicero says, ironically, he avoided by fleeing from the city: the first phrase (diem <foreign xml:lang="lat">dixerat</foreign>) refers to a notice of an accusation before the public assembly; the second (<foreign xml:lang="lat">multam</foreign> <foreign xml:lang="lat">inrogarat</foreign>), to a bill for a fine; the third (<foreign xml:lang="lat">actionem perduellionis</foreign>), to an action on a capital charge before the <foreign xml:lang="lat">comitia centuriata</foreign>. <milestone n="187.2" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="servorum_36">, etc.: the facts, as opposed to the ironical statement that precedes. Cicero had really to fear, not judicial proceedings instituted by Clodius, but mob violence instigated by him. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vidi_enim_37"> , <gloss>I saw with my own eyes</gloss>. Cicero here artfully recounts other violent acts of Clodius, in the form of reasons which moved him, — thus killing two birds with one stone. <milestone n="187.5" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Hortensium_37">: Cicero's early rival, and opponent in the case of Verres. He was now one of Milo's counsel. <milestone n="187.7" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Vibienus_37">: probably a lapse of Cicero's memory. He was killed in the riots after the death of Clodius. <milestone n="187.10" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="haec_37">, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="huic_37">, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="haec_37_2">: notice the emphatic repetition (<foreign xml:lang="lat">anaphora</foreign> ). <milestone n="187.12" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Papiri_37">: see note on sect. IS (p. 178,1. IS). <milestone n="187.14" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ad_regiam_37">: the old palace of Numa on the <foreign xml:lang="lat">Sacra Via</foreign> at the point where it reached the Forum. It adjoined the temple of Vesta, and was occupied by the <foreign xml:lang="lat">pontifex maximus</foreign> (at this time Caesar). When Augustus was made <foreign xml:lang="lat">pontifex maximus</foreign> he gave the Regia to the Vestal Virgins. The occasion here referred to was probably an election riot in the preceding year. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quid_38">, etc., <gloss>what like deed of Milo's?</gloss> <milestone n="187.16" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="detrahi_posset_38">, on account of the disturbances and lawlessness of the time. <pb n="445"/> <milestone n="187.19" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="potuitne_38"> = <foreign xml:lang="lat">nonne potuit</foreign>: § <ref n="AG 332">332</ref>, c (210, d); B. 162, 2, c; G.454 R2; H-B 231,1, b, N.1. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="deos_penatis_38">: see note, Cat. 4, sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 4.18">18</ref> (<ref n="pl151.5">p. 151, l. 5</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fllo_oppugnante_38">: this was an attack not by a mob but by an armed band, made upon Milo's house, November 12, <date when="-0057">B.C. 57</date>, the year of Cicero's return. <milestone n="187.22" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Fabricio_38">: Q. Fabricius, tribune <date when="-0057">B.C. 57</date>; be was the originator and defender of the bill for Cicero's return from exile; an attack was made upon him by the partisans of Clodius, and he barely escaped with his life. <milestone n="187.23" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Caecili_38">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">praetor</foreign> <date when="-0057">B.C. 57</date>. He was attacked while presiding over the games of Apollo in July. <milestone n="187.25" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="lata_lex_38">, i.e. the law proposed for his recall. <milestone n="187.26" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="facti_38">, i.e. the killing of Clodius. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="consensus_39"> , <gloss>universal feeling</gloss>. <milestone n="188.3" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="praetores_39">: all except Appius Claudius, brother of Clodius; <foreign xml:lang="lat"> tribuni</foreign>: all except two, Numerius Rufus and Sex. Atilius Serranus. <milestone n="188.4" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="auctor_39">, the responsible originator; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dux_39">, <gloss> champion</gloss>, who led it to a successful issue. <milestone n="188.7" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="decretum_39">: this word is sometimes used for the proclamation of a magistrate, which was properly <foreign xml:lang="lat">edictum</foreign>. The <foreign xml:lang="lat">decretum</foreign> was the ordinance of a <foreign xml:lang="lat">collegium</foreign> or council, especially of the Senate, but also of any municipal body. The decree here referred to was passed by the municipal Senate (<foreign xml:lang="lat">curia</foreign>) of Capua, upon Pompey's proposition. <milestone n="183.11" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qui_ejus_39"> , <gloss>of any one who</gloss>, equivalent to a conditional construction: § <ref n="AG 519">519</ref> (316); B. 312, 2; G. 593, i ; H. (507, iii, 2); H.-B. 577. <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="gravissimam_partem_40"> , <gloss>a most important part in political affairs</gloss>. <milestone n="188.21" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

Mil. 23">23</ref>) is obvious. <milestone n="190.2" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="facultas_45">: omit, as being a gloss. <milestone n="190.7" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eadem_hora_46">: in the famous trial of the violation of the mysteries (sect. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 13">13</ref>) Clodius had tried to prove an <foreign xml:lang="lat">alibi</foreign> by showing, from Causinius's testimony, that he had spent that night at his house at Interamna (Terni, on the river Nar in Umbria, ninety miles away); but he was confuted by the evidence of Cicero, who testified that he had seen him in Rome the same day. This act of Cicero's Clodius never forgot or forgave. <milestone n="190.21" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="mansurum_fuisse_46">: § <ref n="AG 589">589</ref>, b, 2 (337, b, 2); B. 322; G. 597, R.4 ; H. 647 (527, iii); H.-B. 581, b, I the <foreign xml:lang="lat">protasis</foreign> is suppressed but may be supplied from the <foreign xml:lang="lat">sed</foreign> clause following. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="liberatur_47"> , <gloss>is proved</gloss> (lit. <gloss>is cleared</gloss>). <milestone n="190.26" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="rogatione_47">, i.e. Pompey's law to establish this court of inquiry. <milestone n="190.30" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quem_pridie_48">, etc., i.e. Clodius, when he left Rome, knew that Cyrus was dying, and so the news of his actual death need not have changed his plans. Observe the skill with which every circumstance is made to tell in favor of Cicero's contention that Clodius lay in wait for Milo. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="age_49">, <gloss>well then</gloss>; sit, etc., <gloss>suppose it were so</gloss> (that the messenger informed him about Cyrus). <milestone n="191.16" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="properato_49">: § <ref n="AG 411">411</ref>,a(243, e, N.); B. 218, 2,c; G. 406; H.477, iii (414, iv, N.3); H.-B. 430, 2. <milestone n="191.21" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="insidiator_49">: ironical, as before. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_ut_51"> (see note on <foreign xml:lang="lat">rogasset</foreign>, <ref n="pl190.10">p. 190, l. 10</ref>), <gloss>now though</gloss> (cf. <foreign xml:lang="lat">quod si</foreign>). <milestone n="192.3" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="constare_omnia_51">, etc.: in this and the following section Cicero sums up the arguments given in detail in sects. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 32">32</ref>-<ref n="Cic. Mil. 51">51</ref>. The review is intended to show that, up to the moment of the affray, all the circumstances point to Clodius and not to Milo as the <foreign xml:lang="lat">insidiator</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nihil_umquam_52">, etc.: on the contrary, Cicero says elsewhere (Att. iv, 3), speaking of the disorder that followed his return from exile, "If he [Clodius] comes in his way, I foresee that he will be killed by Milo. <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="etiam_53">, <gloss>any longer</gloss>. <milestone n="192.21" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tarde_54">, etc.: cf. sect. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 49">49</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qui_54"> (adv.) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="convenit_54"> , <gloss>what fitness in that?</gloss> <milestone n="193.7" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hic_54">, i.e. Milo. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="veniret_54">: § <ref n="AG 553">553</ref> (328); B. 293, iii, 2; G. 572; H. 603, ii, 2 (519, ii, 2); H.-B. 507,5. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pransi_56">: the <foreign xml:lang="lat">prandium</foreign> was the noon-day meal, generally simple, of fruit and bread, but made by high-livers a luxurious meal. <milestone n="193.29" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="manu_misit_57">: here Cicero pauses to reply to an argument on the other side. Only slaves could be forced to give testimony by torture (Rosc. Am., sect. <ref n="Cic. S. Rosc. 35">35</ref>). As Milo had freed his, it was maintained by the prosecution that he wished to destroy evidence. <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="indagamus_hic_57">, i.e. the legal aspect of the case is to be considered at this point. <milestone n="194.10" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="id_58">, i.e. the service of saving his life. </gloss>
