<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quaestiones_59"> , <gloss>examination</gloss> (by torture) of Clodius's slaves. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="urgent_59">, i.e. is a difficult point for the defence to meet. <milestone n="194.26" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_Libertatis_59">: it was in this hall (probably near the present column of Trajan) that questions touching the liberation of slaves were considered, and that torture was inflicted, — not in mockery of the name, but to excite in the slave some hope of freedom. <milestone n="194.27" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Appius_59">: an elder brother of Clodius. <milestone n="194.29" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="de_servis_59">: the passage in brackets seems necessary to the sense. The exception <foreign xml:lang="lat">de incestu</foreign> is mentioned to bring the jest upon Clodius. <milestone n="194.30" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="proxime_59">, <gloss>very near</gloss>: i.e. by having his murder treated as sacrilege (i.e. an offence against the gods) in respect to the question of slaves. The whole passage is an argument <foreign xml:lang="lat">a fortiori</foreign>. If the Romans excluded enforced testimony of a master's slaves, even when the truth could be arrived at thereby, how much more should this be excluded here, where the temptation to lie was so great that no trustworthy evidence could be expected from them. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="deos_accessit_59"> is punningly used in two senses, — first, of his near approach to being a god (by having an offence against him treated as sacrilege); second, of his unlawful intrusion on the mysteries of the Bona Dea. <milestone n="194.31" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ad_ipsos_59">, i.e. in the mysteries of Bona Dea (<ref n="pl176.14">p. 176, l. 14</ref>, note). <milestone n="195.3" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_quin_59"> , <gloss>not but</gloss>: § <ref n="AG 540">540</ref>, N.3 (321, R.); B. 286,1, b; G. 541, N.2; H. 588, ii, 2 (516, 2); H-B. 535, 2, b. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cave_mentiaris_60">: § <ref n="AG 450">450</ref>, 2 (269,a,3); G. 271,2; H. 561, 2 (489, 2); H-B. 501, 3,0, 2. <milestone n="195.11" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="arcas_60">, <gloss> cells</gloss>, lit. <gloss>chests</gloss> (of timber), which in more ancient times were apparently used for this purpose. <milestone n="195.14" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="integrius_60"> , <gloss>sounder</gloss>, i.e. less biased (of course ironical). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Romam_revertisse_61">: it was at first thought that Milo had gone into voluntary exile after the murder. In fact, however, he had returned to Rome on the night when the <foreign xml:lang="lat">curia</foreign> was burned, and the reaction caused by the fire and the riots encouraged him to appear in public and renew his canvass for the consulship. Cicero skillfully represents this conduct as due to the courage of innocence. <milestone n="195.22" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="se_commisit_61">, i.e. by appearing in his place among them. <milestone n="195.23" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="praesidils_61">, i.e. the special power with which Pompey was clothed as sole consul, which is further dwelt on in the following (see sect. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 65">65</ref>). <milestone n="195.28" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="magna_metuenti_61">, etc.: Pompey was on friendly terms with Clodius and might well fear that the violent act of Milo threatened his own position in the state and even his life (see note on sect. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 66">66</ref>, <ref n="pl197.26">p. 197, l. 26</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="imperitorum_62"> , <gloss>strangers</gloss> to his character (though well intentioned). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illud_63">, the <gloss>fact</gloss> (in appos. with the clause </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_trucidaret_63"> ). <milestone n="196.8" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fecisset_63">: for <gloss>fecit</gloss> of the dir. disc.; so <foreign xml:lang="lat"> voluisset</foreign> in l. 11, below. <milestone n="196.9" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tanti_63">, predicate gen. of indefinite value. <milestone n="196.12" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fortem_virum_63"> , <gloss>brave man that he was</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quin_cederet_63"> , </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="auferret_63"> , </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="relinqueret_63">: § <ref n="AG 558">558</ref>, a, N.2 (332, g, N.2); B. 298, b; G. 555, R.3; H. 596, I (505, i); cf. H.-B. 502, 31 b and footnote 4. <milestone n="196.16" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="portenta_63"> , <gloss>monsters</gloss> (his accomplices). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="loquebantur_63"> , <gloss>talked about</gloss>, comparing Milo with Catiline, and saying he would do likewise. <milestone n="196.17" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="miseros_civis_63">, exclamatory accusative. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illa_64">, <gloss>these surmises</gloss>. <milestone n="196.24" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="perculissent_64">: the <foreign xml:lang="lat">protasis</foreign> is implied in </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quemvis_conscientia_64"> (i.e. anybody who had such a consciousness). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="conscientia_64">, abl. of means with <foreign xml:lang="lat">perculissent</foreign>. <milestone n="196.25" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_64"> (exclamatory), <gloss>how</gloss>. <milestone n="196.26" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="maximo_animo_64"> , <gloss>of the greatest hardihood</gloss>. <milestone n="196.27" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="potuisset_64">: the <foreign xml:lang="lat">protasis</foreign> is implied in the context. <milestone n="196.29" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="indicabatur_64">: § <ref n="AG 582">582</ref> (330, b, 1); cf. B. 332; G. 528, H. 611, N.1 (534, I, 11.1); H.-B. 590, I ; use the impers. form in translation, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="it_that_64">, etc. <milestone n="196.30" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vicum_64">, <gloss>narrow street</gloss> (properly a <gloss>district</gloss> or <gloss>quarter</gloss> ). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dicebant_64"> <gloss>they would say</gloss> (indicating repeated charges). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Miloni_64">, dat. of agent: § <ref n="AG 374">374</ref> (232); B. 189, 2; G. 354; H. 431, 2 (388, I); H-B. 373, 2. <milestone n="197.2" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="clivo_Capitolino_64">, the street which ran from the upper end of the Forum to the <gloss> Capitolium</gloss>. <milestone n="197.4" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="delata_64">: <foreign xml:lang="lat">deferre ad senatum</foreign> is to lay information before the Senate; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="referre_64">, to bring a piece of business before it for action. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="laudabam_65">, imperf. because it indicates Cicero's <gloss>state of mind</gloss> at the time. <milestone n="197.6" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sed_dicam_65">, etc.: the whole context implies that in this matter Pompey went too far, and for this Cicero excuses him in what follows. <milestone n="197.8" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fuit_audiendus_65"> , <gloss>he had to listen to</gloss> (not cont. to fact). <milestone n="197.9" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="popa_65">, an inferior priest who slew the sacrifices — hardly more than a butcher: such a person usually (as here) kept <foreign xml:lang="lat">apopina</foreign>, or restaurant and grog-shop; hence <foreign xml:lang="lat">apud se ebrios</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Circo_maximo_65">: the district near the Circus Maximus, the building for the great games, between the Palatine and Aventine hills. <pb n="453"/> <milestone n="197.12" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_hortos_65">: § <ref n="AG 428">428</ref>, 1, N. (259, g); H. 418 (380, i); wealthy Romans had large grounds (<foreign xml:lang="lat">horti</foreign>) attached to their city houses. Pompey was at this time staying at home to avoid Milo, as was alleged. <milestone n="197.16" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="credi_popae_65">: § <ref n="AG 372">372</ref> (230); B. 187, ii, b; G. 346, R.1; H. 426, 3 (384, ii, 5); H.-B. 364, 2. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tam_loco_66"> , <gloss>in so thronged a locality</gloss>. Caesar, as Pontifex Maximus, inhabited the <foreign xml:lang="lat">Begia</foreign> (see note on sect. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 37">37</ref>, <ref n="pl187.14">p. 187, l. 14</ref>) on the <foreign xml:lang="lat">Sacra Via</foreign>, in the busiest part of Rome. <milestone n="197.23" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="audiebatur_66"> , <gloss>the story was told</gloss>. <milestone n="197.26" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="senator_66">, etc.: "Pompey was afraid of Milo, or pretended to be; and he stayed mostly, not at home, but in his gardens — even the upper ones, where a great guard of soldiers camped around. Pompey, besides, had once adjourned the Senate suddenly, saying that he feared Milo's coming. Then at the next session, P. Cornificius had said that Milo had a sword under his tunic, fastened to his thigh, and demanded that he should bare his thigh, which Milo did at once, lifting his tunic. Then Cicero called out that all the other charges against Milo were just like that." (Asconius.) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_perhorrescimus_67">: § <ref n="AG 549">549</ref>, a (326, a); B. 290, I; G. 582; H. 599 (517, 2); H.-B. 550, a, N.1. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tamen_67">, i.e. notwithstanding the alleged grounds for fearing Milo have been proved false. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_metuitur_67">: the <foreign xml:lang="lat">protasis</foreign> is </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="timemus_67">, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="perhorrescimus_67"> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="etiam_nunc_67">, opposed to the instances of such fear of Milo mentioned in the preceding chapter. <milestone n="197.32" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Clodianum_crimen_67"> , <gloss>the charge of murdering Clodius</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="timemus_67_2">, <gloss> we</gloss> = Cicero and his client: they have to fear, he says, — in case Milo is still regarded by Pompey with apprehension, — not the charge of murdering Clodius (for Milo is sure of an acquittal if the case is decided on its merits) but Pompey's suspicions of Milo's hostility to <gloss>him</gloss> (for these may well prove prejudicial to Milo's case). These suspicions Cicero answers by a <foreign xml:lang="lat">reductio ad absurdum,</foreign> — if all these military preparations have been made for fear of Milo, what a great man Milo must be! But everybody knows that they were not made for this reason; hence Pompey has no reason to fear Milo. <milestone n="198.1" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="exaudire_67">: Pompey was sitting not in the court but at the Treasury, a considerable distance off. <milestone n="198.2" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_putas_67">, etc.: the apod. is </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="magna_hoc_67">, etc., 1.9. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sed_quis_68"> , <gloss>but</gloss> [this cannot be, for] <gloss>who</gloss>, etc. <milestone n="198.14" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_locus_68">, etc., i.e. if Milo had had a chance, he would have proved his devotion to Pompey. <milestone n="198.18" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illa_peste_68">, i.e. Clodius. <milestone n="198.19" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tribunatum_68">: Milo was tribune <date when="-0057">B.C. 57</date>, and he was active in securing the recall of Cicero from exile. <milestone n="198.23" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quae_non_68">, etc., <gloss>if he could not prove this</gloss>. <milestone n="198.25" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="armis_conquietura_68"> , <gloss>were never likely to rest front arms</gloss>. <milestone n="198.26" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ne_68">, <gloss> assuredly</gloss>. <milestone n="198.27" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ita_natus_68"> , <gloss>born for that very thing</gloss> (i.e. to sacrifice everything for his country). <milestone n="198.28" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="te_antestaretur_68"> , <gloss>would call you to witness</gloss> (that he yields to the occasion and is really guiltless of any hostilities). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="infidelitates_69">, [acts of] <gloss>ill-faith</gloss>. This and the following plural abstracts are, as often, best translated by the singular in English: § <ref n="AG 100">100</ref>, c (75, c) ; B. 551 4, c; G. 204, R5; H. 138, 2 (130, 2); H.-B. 240,5, b. <milestone n="199.2" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="motu_aliquo_69">, i.e. there will perhaps be some disturbance of the general welfare of the state (<foreign xml:lang="lat">communium temporum</foreign>). Cicero's apprehensions were more than fulfilled in the great Civil War between Caesar and Pompey. <milestone n="199.4" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="experti_69">: an allusion to Cicero's own misfortunes and exile. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quamquam_70">: corrective, implying that the preceding supposition is unfounded, for Pompey is not really an enemy to Milo; if he had been, he would have executed him summarily and not have allowed him a trial. His action, Cicero argues, virtually acquits the defendant. <milestone n="199.7" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="juris_publici_70">, etc., law, customs, politics. <milestone n="199.9" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ne_quid_70">, etc.: see note, Cat. 1, sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 1.2">2</ref> (<ref n="pl100.12">p. 100, l. 12</ref>). <milestone n="199.11" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hunc_70"> repeats </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Pompeium_70"> with emphasis after the long parenthesis; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ejus_qui_70"> , <gloss>of one who</gloss> (on that supposition), i.e. Milo. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dilectu_70">: Pompey held the consulship in <date when="-0055">B.C. 55</date>, but after its expiration did not go into his province of Spain, but despatched thither his army under the command of <foreign xml:lang="lat"> legati</foreign>, while he himself remained in Italy with <foreign xml:lang="lat">proconsular</foreign> power. Immediately after the death of Clodius the Senate gave the <foreign xml:lang="lat">interrex</foreign> (see note, <ref n="pl176.18">p. 176, l. 18</ref>), the tribunes, and the proconsul (Pompey) the extraordinary power <foreign xml:lang="lat"> <emph>ne quid</emph> </foreign>, etc., and authorized Pompey to hold a levy of troops. <milestone n="199.12" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="exspectaturum_fuisse_70"> , <gloss>would have</gloss>, etc.: § <ref n="AG 589">589</ref>, b, 2 (337, b, 2); B. 322; G. 659; H. 647(527, iii); H.-B. 581, b, I. The whole passage is a cont. to fact apod. in indir. disc.; the <foreign xml:lang="lat">protasis</foreign> (implied in the context) is the false supposition that Pompey thought Milo dangerous to the state and to himself. <pb n="455"/> <milestone n="199.14" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ista_70">, i.e. the imputations referred to in sects. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 65">65</ref> , <ref n="Cic. Mil. 66">66</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qui_70">, i.e. Pompey. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="legem_70">, <gloss>the law</gloss> for the present investigation. <milestone n="199.1" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="s_oporteret_70"> , <gloss>ought, as I think</gloss>; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="liceret_70">, <gloss>may well</gloss> (legally), <gloss>as all allow</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_loco_71">: see note on <foreign xml:lang="lat">exaudire</foreign>, sect. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 67">67</ref>, <ref n="pl198.1">p. 198, l. 1</ref>. <milestone n="199.20" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

(contrary to the sequence of tenses) stands for <foreign xml:lang="lat">poterat</foreign> and implies that the action did not take place: § <ref n="AG 522">522</ref>, a (311, c); B. 304,3, a; G. 254, R.2; H. 525, I (476, 4); 14.-B. 582, 3, a. <milestone n="199.21" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hesternam_contionem_71">: Cf. sect. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 3">3</ref> (<ref n="pl172.3">p. 172, l. 3</ref> ). </gloss>

This <foreign xml:lang="lat">apodosis</foreign> is itself a conditional sentence consisting of a cont. to fact apod. (<foreign xml:lang="lat">dubitaret</foreign>) with a prot. "if he had done it" implied. The argument is as follows: Milo does not deny killing Clodius in <gloss>self-defence</gloss>, which is only an <gloss>excuse</gloss> for the homicide; if, therefore, he had killed him <gloss>deliberately</gloss>, would he not gladly admit it, since he might justly expect such a service to the state as the removal of this desperado to meet with gratitude from all? Hence Milo's denial is worthy of credence, since it would be for his interest to admit the charge. <milestone n="203.20" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nisi_vero_81">: § <ref n="AG 525">525</ref>, b, N. (315, b, N.) ; Cf. B. 306,5; G. 591, R.4; H.-B. 578, 3,a. This clause introduces the ironical supposition that Milo thinks it more pleasing to the citizens for him to have defended <gloss>himself</gloss> in killing Clodius than to have defended <gloss>them</gloss>. Only on this absurd hypothesis, Cicero reasons, could Milo refuse to admit the charge brought against him if he were guilty. <milestone n="203.22" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="commosse_85"> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">commavisse</foreign>) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="se_85">, <gloss>bestirred themselves</gloss>. <milestone n="205.4" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="retinuisse_85"> , <gloss>reasserted</gloss>. <milestone n="205.5" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Albani_85">: Clodius's Alban villa (see sects. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 46">46</ref>,<ref n="Cic. Mil. 51">51</ref>) must have been in the territory of Alba Longa, the ancient capital of Latium, whose temples were spared and their worship adopted by Rome (as that of the Lanuvian Juno had been, see note, sect. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 27">27</ref>) when the city was destroyed. From what follows it would appear that some of these sanctuaries had been demolished by Clodius in his building schemes (see sect. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 53">53</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tumuli_85">, <gloss> mounds</gloss>, used for altars. <milestone n="205.10" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="viguerunt_85"> , <gloss>revived</gloss>. Observe the alliteration. <milestone n="205.11" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Latiaris_85">: the temple of Jupiter on the Alban Mount was the religious <foreign xml:lang="lat">centre</foreign> of the Latin confederacy. <milestone n="205.12" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="lacus_85">: there are several little lakes about the Alban Mount, chief of which are those at Alba and Aricia, in the craters of extinct volcanoes. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nemora_85">: <foreign xml:lang="lat">nemus</foreign> is originally an open grove where cattle can graze; it is applied, as well as <foreign xml:lang="lat"> lucus</foreign>, to a consecrated grove. Of these the most famous in Italy was the sanctuary of Diana on the <foreign xml:lang="lat">Lacus Nemorensis</foreign> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">L. Nemi</foreign>) near Aricia. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Bonae_deae_86">: an Italian goddess who probably represented the fruitful power of the earth, so that her mysteries, celebrated on December 3 and 4, corresponded to those of Demeter (<gloss>Mother Earth</gloss>) at Eleusis. It was her mysteries that Clodius had profaned (see note on sect. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 13">13</ref>, p.176, 1.14). <pb n="461"/> <milestone n="205.20" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="taeterrimam_86">, i.e. as having been slain while engaged in a criminal attempt. <milestone n="205.21" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="judicio_86">: cf. sect. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 13">13</ref> (<ref n="pl176.15">p. 176, l. 15</ref>). <milestone n="205.22" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nec_non_86"> , <gloss>nor can it be but that</gloss> <milestone n="205.24" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="imaginibus_86"> (cf. <foreign xml:lang="lat">formas</foreign>, 1.28), waxen <gloss>masks</gloss> of ancestors, worn by persons in the funeral procession, to represent the departed worthies (see note on the <foreign xml:lang="lat">jus imaginum</foreign>, Introduction, p.1, N.1); </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cantu_86">, <gloss> music</gloss>; <foreign xml:lang="lat">ludis</foreign>, <gloss>games</gloss> ; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="exsequiis_86"> , <gloss>procession</gloss>; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="funere_86">, <gloss>burial rites</gloss>. <milestone n="205.26" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="celebritate_86"> , <gloss>throng</gloss> (see sect. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 33">33</ref>, and notes). <milestone n="205.30" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="mortem_lacerari_86"> , <gloss>that his dead body should be mangled</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_quo_86">, <gloss>that in which</gloss>, i.e. the Forum. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quae_pateretur_87">: § <ref n="AG 535">535</ref>, e (320, e); B. 283, 2; G. 633; 11.592 (517); H.-B. 523. <milestone n="206.3" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="consensu_87">, modifying </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="gesta_87">: the acts referred to are those of Cicero's consulship, which Clodius had practically <gloss>annulled</gloss> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">resciderat</foreign>) by procuring his banishment. <milestone n="206.4" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="domum_incenderat_87">: this was in <date when="-0057">B.C. 57</date>. <milestone n="206.9" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="capere_87">, <gloss> contain</gloss>. <milestone n="206.10" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="incidebantur_87">: he felt so sure of his power that he was having the laws engraved (on bronze tablets, according to the custom) even before their passage. <milestone n="206.11" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nos_addicerent_87"> , <gloss>which should bind us over to our own slaves</gloss> (i.e. freedmen). The suffrage of the freedmen was a standing subject of controversy in Roman politics. They voted in the four city tribes (see note on sect. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 25">25</ref>, <ref n="pl181.26">p. 181, l. 26</ref>), but many efforts were made to get them into the rustic tribes; and Clodus had promised, as praetor, to bring forward a law with this object. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illum_ipsum_88">, i.e. Pompey, who returned to Rome from the East just before the Clodian disturbances began. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="reditu_88">, referring to his reconciliation with Pompey. <milestone n="206.18" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hic_88">, <gloss>at this point</gloss> (in the development of Clodius's plans).—supra: see sect. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 84">84</ref> (<ref n="pl204.30">p. 204, l. 30</ref>), and note. <milestone n="206.19" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aliter_88">, i.e. if he had not laid this plot (fatal to himself) against Milo: virtually the <foreign xml:lang="lat">protasis</foreign> of all the cont. to fact <foreign xml:lang="lat">apodoses</foreign> in this and the following section. <milestone n="206.20" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="potuit_88">, cont. to fact apod.; cf. <foreign xml:lang="lat">esset ulta</foreign> (1.21). <milestone n="206.21" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="circumscripsisset_88">, i.e. <gloss>kept him within the legitimate bounds of his office</gloss> (as <foreign xml:lang="lat">praetor</foreign>): of course ironical. <pb n="462"/> <milestone n="206.22" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="id_facere_88">, i.e. when the Senate used to exercise that power. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_privato_88">, i.e. when he held no magistracy. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="suos_89">, i.e. just the ones whom he wanted. <milestone n="206.26" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="virtutem_consularem_89"> , <gloss>the courage of a consul</gloss> (i.e. Cicero). <milestone n="206.29" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="libertos_suos_89">: if be freed the slaves of others, they would be his freedmen, and bound to him as clients (see note, Rosc. Am., <ref n="pl6.10">p. 6, l. 10</ref> ). <milestone n="206.30" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nisi_impulissent_89">, a formal repetition of the <foreign xml:lang="lat">protasis</foreign> implied in <foreign xml:lang="lat">aliter</foreign> (l.19, above). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="templum_90">, etc., <gloss>the sanctuary of public purity, grandeur, wisdom, and counsel</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="templum_inflammari_90">, indir. disc. after the idea of <gloss>seeing</gloss> continued from <foreign xml:lang="lat">vidimus</foreign>. <milestone n="207.7" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aram_sociorum_90">, as being the place to which they looked for protection. <milestone n="207.8" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="portum_90">, <gloss> haven</gloss> or <gloss>refuge</gloss>. <milestone n="207.9" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="funestari_90"> , <gloss>defiled</gloss> by the presence of a corpse. <milestone n="207.11" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ab_uno_90">, i.e. Sex. Clodius. <milestone n="207.12" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ustor_90">, i.e. in the humble capacity of a mere cremator. A bitter jest on the consequences of Sex. Clodius's act in burning the body. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="via_Appia_91">, where the homicide was committed (cf. sect. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 17">17</ref>, p. 178,l.13). <milestone n="207.15" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ab_eo_91">, <gloss> from</gloss> (i.e. against) <gloss>him</gloss>. <milestone n="207.18" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="furias_91">, virtually = <gloss>madness</gloss>, though with a vague allusion to the Furies, who drove a guilty man on to further crimes. <milestone n="207.20" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="falcibus_91"> , <gloss>hooks</gloss> (like firemen's hooks) to tear up the steps and turn the building into a fortress. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ad_Castoris_91">: see note, sect. 18 (<ref n="pl178.25">p. 178, l. 25</ref>). <milestone n="207.21" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="disturbari_91"> , <gloss>broken up</gloss> (not merely "disturbed"). <milestone n="207.22" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="silentio_91">, i.e. the <foreign xml:lang="lat">contio</foreign> was orderly arid well disposed until the attack of the Clodians. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="M_Caelius_91">: a young man who was esteemed by Cicero as of great promise, and defended by him in a cause of some scandal, but who afterwards turned out to be a wild and desperate demagogue. In the year <date when="-0044">B.C. 44</date>, after Caesar's victory at Pharsalia, both Caelius and Milo, in concert with each other, headed revolts against Caesar, and lost their lives ignominiously in southern Italy. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="orationem_92"> , <gloss>manner of speech</gloss>. <milestone n="208.3" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hoc_92">, <gloss>on this account</gloss>: § <ref n="AG 414">414</ref>, a, N. (250, N.); B. 223; G. 403; 11.479 (423); Cf. H.-B. 424, a. <milestone n="208.5" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="infimi_fortuna_92"> , <gloss>in regard to the lot and fortunes of men of the lowest class</gloss> (i.e. the gladiators). <milestone n="208.6" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_obsecrantis_92"> (acc. plur.). <milestone n="208.8" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cupimus_92">: in gladiatorial contests, if one combatant bad the other at his mercy, he waited the will of the people, who expressed their wishes by turning their thumbs up or down. If most thumbs were turned up, he was put to death. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="exanimant_93">, etc., <gloss>these words of Milo dishearten and depress me</gloss>. <milestone n="208.14" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="valeant_93">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">valeant</foreign>: translate (to preserve the lit. meaning of the word, which is here played upon) <gloss>good-bye to my fellow-citizens, may heaven bless them!</gloss> <milestone n="208.17" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="licet_93">, Sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">perfrui</foreign>. <milestone n="208.18" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="propter_me_93"> , <gloss>through my efforts</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="re_oppressa_94"> , <gloss>when the government was crushed</gloss>. <milestone n="208.24" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="acceperam_94">, had <foreign xml:lang="lat">found</foreign>. <milestone n="208.26" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Clodianis_armis_94"> (abl. of cause), i.e. on account of the violence of Clodius. <milestone n="208.27" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="putarem_94">, <gloss>should I have thought?</gloss> § <ref n="AG 444">444</ref> and N. (268 and R.); B. 227; G. 466; 11.559, 4 (484, v); H.-B. 503. <milestone n="208.31" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tui_94">: Cicero was of an equestrian family and throughout his career favored the interests of the <foreign xml:lang="lat">equites</foreign> and was supported by them in turn (cf. Manil., sect. <ref n="Cic. Man. 4">4</ref> ). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quo_videtis_95">, sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">eum esse</foreign>. <milestone n="209.5" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ingratis_civibus_95"> , <gloss>that it is for <emph>ungrateful</emph> fellow-citizens that he has</gloss>, etc.: the Latin, from its freedom in the use of emphatic position, is able to say this in a single clause. <milestone n="209.8" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="se_ut_95">, etc., <gloss>that he has succeeded in</gloss>, etc. <milestone n="209.9" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tribus_patrimonus_95">: Milo was by birth a member of the Papian <foreign xml:lang="lat">gens</foreign>, but was adopted by his maternal grandfather, C. Annius. This accounts for two patrimonies; the third, Asconius thinks, was probably his mother's. The orator here makes a civic virtue out of Milo's lavish bribery. <milestone n="209.10" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ne_conciliarit_95"> , <gloss>that he has not won over</gloss>: § <ref n="AG 564">564</ref> (331 f); B. 295, 2, a; G. 550, 2; 11.567, 2 (498, iii, N.2); H.-B. 502, 4. <milestone n="209.15" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ablaturum_95"> , <gloss>shall tear away</gloss> (i.e. the memory of them). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vocem_praeconis_96">, etc.: i.e. the election was practically decided when the <foreign xml:lang="lat">comitia</foreign> were broken up by a mob. The election could not therefore be formally and legally complete, and no announcement could be made by the herald, but the will of the people had been already expressed. <milestone n="209.18" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_haec_96">, etc., <gloss>this case shall go against him</gloss>. <milestone n="209.19" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="facinoris_suspicionem_96">, etc., <gloss>the suspicion of a great crime, not the indictment for this act</gloss> That is, as the last chapters have shown, it was, in Cicero's view, not Clodius's death, but suspicion of designs against Pompey and the state, that decided the case against Milo. <milestone n="209.22" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="recte_facta_96">: § <ref n="AG 32">32</ref>r, b (207, c); G. 437, R.; H.-B. 250, 2, b, N. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qui_beneficio_97">, etc., <gloss>who hove surpassed their fellow-citizens in good services</gloss>, i.e. have done more for their fellow-citizens than the latter have repaid by gratitude. <milestone n="209.27" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_ratio_97">, (<gloss>if regard were to be had</gloss> (cont. to fact, implying that it is not a question of rewards at all): to this prot. we should expect an apod. of corresponding form; but this (which would be "we should say," or the like) is supplanted by a simple acc. and inf. of indir. disc. (<foreign xml:lang="lat">amplissimum esse</foreign>, etc.) expressing that which we "should say." <milestone n="209.29" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="consolaretur_97">: this and the following imperfects are due to the change in sequence effected by <foreign xml:lang="lat">esset habenda</foreign> above. This change of tense, however, is only formal, and the imperfect may be translated by the present. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="faces_98">, etc.: for the figure, cf. Cat. 1, sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 1.29">29</ref> . <milestone n="210.5" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Etruriae_dies_98">: holidays appointed by the people of Etruria, whom Clodius had cheated and robbed, at the good news of his death. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="et_institutos_98">, agreeing with <foreign xml:lang="lat">festos</foreign> dies: the celebrations that have already taken place, and the anniversaries that have been established. <milestone n="210.7" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qua_en_98"> , <gloss>wherever. . . there</gloss> (abl. of way by which). <milestone n="210.9" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_laboro_98"> , <gloss>I have no concern</gloss>. <milestone n="210.10" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="versatur_98"> , <gloss>abides</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="his_99">, i.e. those present at the trial. <milestone n="210.13" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_es_99">: Cf. <foreign xml:lang="lat">cum timemus</foreign>, sect. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 67">67</ref> (<ref n="pl197.31">p. 197, l. 31</ref>). <milestone n="210.17" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_possim_99">: § <ref n="AG 571">571</ref>, c (332,f); G. 557, a.; 11. 571, 4 (501, iii); cf. H.-B. 521, 3, a, footnote2. <milestone n="210.22" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quanti_99">, pred. gen. of indefinite value. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quae_oblivio_99"> , <gloss>forgetfulness of this</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pietatis_100"> , <gloss>gratitude</gloss>. <milestone n="210.28" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="inimicitias_100">, etc.: "Such," says Asconius, "were the constancy and good faith of Cicero, that neither the popular enmity, nor the suspicions of Pompey, nor the fear of coming danger if he should be put on trial before the people, nor the arms openly taken up against Milo, could deter him from his defence, when he might have shunned all danger and popular wrath, and even won back the good will of Pompey, by relaxing a little the zeal of his advocacy." </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hic_mente_101">, etc., i.e. this self-renunciation is in accordance with Milo's character, but (it is implied) the jurors ought not to be so affected by it as to refuse to acquit him. <milestone n="211.13" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="excipiat_101">, after <foreign xml:lang="lat">dignior qui</foreign>: § <ref n="AG 535"> 535</ref>, f (320, f); B. 282, 3; G. 631, I; 11.591, 7 (503, ii 2); H.-B. 513, 3. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hos_102">, i.e. the Roman citizens present. <milestone n="211.23" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="temporum_illorum_102">, i.e. the time of Cicero's distress and banishment. <milestone n="211.24" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="mene_potuisse_102">, sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">respondebo</foreign>. <milestone n="211.26" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="gentibus_102">: a line must haye dropped out, part of which belongs with <foreign xml:lang="lat">gentibus</foreign>. The meaning of the rest of the section is therefore not clear. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illa_indicia_103">, i.e. those of Catiline's conspiracy. <milestone n="212.2" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fuerit_103">, subj. of integral part. <milestone n="212.3" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="possum_103">, virtually future, and so used as <foreign xml:lang="lat">apodosis</foreign> to a future <foreign xml:lang="lat">protasis</foreign>. <milestone n="212.6" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dixerim_103">: § <ref n="AG 446">446</ref> (311, a); B. 280, I; G. 257; H. 552 (485); H.-B. 519, I, b. <milestone n="212.8" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dictator_103">: in times of great public emergency the Senate could call upon the consuls to create a Dictator, who should possess the undivided power of the old kings, but only for the period of six months. The laws of appeal and other safeguards of individual liberty had at first no force against this magistrate. In later times dictators were no longer appointed, but the consuls were invested with dictatorial power by the formula, <foreign xml:lang="lat">videant ne</foreign>, etc. (Cat. 1, sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 1.2">2</ref>). Sulla, and afterwards Caesar, revived the name and authority of this magistracy, <pb n="466"/> but, by holding it for life (<foreign xml:lang="lat">perpetuo</foreign>), completely changed its character, making it equivalent to absolute sovereignty. The <foreign xml:lang="lat">Magister Equitum</foreign>, appointed by the Dictator, stood next in command to him. <milestone n="212.9" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="viderem_103">: § <ref n="AG 571">571</ref>, a(332, b); B. 284,4; G. 298 and N.2; H. 570, I (502, 2); H.-B. 507, 4, d. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_Italia_104">: since the Social War, the towns of Italy, having received Roman citizenship, had lost the <foreign xml:lang="lat">jus exsilii</foreign>, i.e. had ceased to be foreign territory to which exiles from Rome could retire. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="lacrimis_defendi_105">: this was a peculiarly Roman custom. Many a desperate case was gained in the Roman courts by putting on mourning and bringing out the wife and children of the accused in deep mourning and bathed in tears. </gloss>
